title: Diagnostic Imaging of the Neonate
authors: Berlin SC, Carr CB / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Diagnostic Imaging?
Neonatologist ไม่ใช่ radiologist — แต่ เราอ่าน X-ray เองทุกวัน และ ตัดสินใจ clinical จาก image โดยตรง. (1) CXR ทุกใบที่สั่ง: ต้องตรวจ ETT / UVC / UAC / PICC position ก่อนแปลผล lung — malposition UAC → aortic branch thrombosis; malposition UVC → portal vein thrombosis. (2) Pneumothorax tension = emergency: ต้องเห็นสัญญาณก่อน radiologist รายงาน; lung US (POCUS) ให้ diagnosis เร็วกว่า CXR ในมือที่ฝึกมา. (3) NEC: pneumatosis intestinalis → portal venous gas → pneumoperitoneum = progression timeline ที่ต้องติดตามทุก 6h. (4) Head US ทำ bedside ทุก wk ใน preterm <32 wk — GMH-IVH grading เป็น prognostic tool ที่เราต้องอธิบายกับครอบครัว. (5) Radiation safety: single portable CXR = 1 วันของ background radiation — ไม่ต้องกลัว แต่สั่งเฉพาะ medically necessary; gonadal shielding ไม่แนะนำอีกต่อไป (AAPM 2019). ทักษะนี้ช่วยชีวิตได้โดยตรง.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
เปรียบเทียบ imaging modalities: radiography, fluoroscopy, CT, US, MRI, scintigraphy, POCUS — ข้อดีข้อเสียใน NICU context
อธิบาย radiation safety: BERT, ALARA, dose per study, gonadal shielding policy ปัจจุบัน
ระบุตำแหน่งที่ถูกต้องของ ETT, UVC, UAC (high/low), PICC (upper/lower extremity), ECMO catheters บน CXR + AXR
แปลผล CXR ใน respiratory conditions: RDS, PIE, pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum (thymic sail), pneumopericardium (halo), TTN, MAS, BPD
ระบุ imaging findings ใน surgical respiratory conditions: CDH, CPAM (types 0–4), CLO, BPS, EA/TEF
วิเคราะห์ AXR ใน GI emergencies: high vs low obstruction, NEC (pneumatosis, portal venous gas, football sign), biliary atresia (triangular cord sign)
อธิบาย cranial US grading ของ GMH-IVH (grades 1–4), PVL stages, HIE findings, LSV — และ clinical implication ต่อ outcome
ระบุ POCUS applications ใน NICU: cardiac (TNE), lung, abdomen, vascular access, lumbar puncture — ข้อจำกัดใน US practice
อธิบาย UTD classification และ management algorithm สำหรับ postnatal hydronephrosis
ระบุ imaging ที่เหมาะสมสำหรับ: biliary atresia workup, DDH screening, occult spinal dysraphism, neuroblastoma staging
1. Imaging Modalities — Overview
1.1 Digital Radiography, Fluoroscopy, CT

Radiation Safety — BERT, ALARA, และ Gonadal Shielding
Background Equivalent Radiation Time (BERT): Natural background = 3 mSv/year (range 1–20 mSv). Portable CXR = 0.01–0.02 mSv ≈ 1 day background radiation. Upper GI series = 0.5 mSv ≈ 2 months; VCUG = 0.3 mSv ≈ 5 weeks; CT head = 0.5–2 mSv ≈ 2–8 months. Serial exams: risk is NOT cumulative (incremental delivery → tissue recovery). Even the highest total NICU radiation stay < annual background. Current epidemiological evidence: no confirmed causal relationship between low-dose (<100 mSv) radiation and cancer. ALARA: size-based tube current/voltage; collimation; iterative reconstruction for CT. Gonadal shielding: DISCONTINUED (AAPM 2019) — shields obscure anatomy, trigger auto-exposure increase (↑ dose), provide no benefit; gonads less radiosensitive than previously thought.
CT without sedation: acquisition time <5 sec → most neonates scanned awake

Iodinated contrast media (ICM): no ↑ renal toxicity in neonates without renal failure; rare transient hypothyroidism — FDA recommends monitoring <3 yr; ACR: risk 'quite low' <3 months, 'minimal to absent' ≥3 months — patient-by-patient basis (not universal testing)
Scatter radiation: at 1 m from primary beam = 0.1–0.2% of incident dose; staff inside 1 m → lead apron
1.2 Ultrasound
Most common modality after radiography; no ionizing radiation; portable; dynamic cine + Doppler — ideal for NICU
Limitations: poor through air/bone; lower spatial resolution than CT/MRI; highly operator-dependent
POCUS (point-of-care ultrasound): bedside clinician-performed for focused clinical question; complements but does NOT replace comprehensive cardiology/radiology exams
US credentialing: not yet standardized in US; more formalized in Australia (ASUM). Major barrier to broad POCUS adoption in US NICUs (Mirza 2017; Nguyen 2016)
1.3 MRI
Feed-and-swaddle technique: majority of infants <3 months scanned without sedation (Heller 2017; Templeton 2020)

Gadolinium-based contrast agents (GBCAs): no renal toxicity without renal failure; gadolinium deposition in brain with repeated doses — no known adverse consequences, but 'prudent to consider benefit vs unknown risk' per ACR 2021
Infant hypothermia during MRI: common if not proactively managed (Don Paul 2018) — swaddle + cap + MRI-compatible isolette
In-NICU MRI scanners: dedicated neonatal scanners housed in NICU — avoid transport risks; diagnostic yield similar to conventional 3T MRI (Thiim 2022)
MRI is gold standard: congenital anomalies, HIE (DWI), PVL, cardiac complex anatomy, spinal dysraphism
1.4 Scintigraphy
Nuclear medicine; nonallergenic, no toxic effects; dose 0.6–5.5 mSv (similar to CT)
Applications: biliary atresia (hepatobiliary scintigraphy), neuroblastoma (123I-MIBG), UTI/reflux (MAG3 renal scan), bone metastases
Most neonates imaged without sedation; requires transport to nuclear medicine suite
2. Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) — Applications
2.1 Cardiac POCUS (Targeted Neonatal Echocardiography — TNE)
Indications: myocardial function assessment, hemodynamically significant PDA, pulmonary hypertension signs, volume status, cardiac tamponade/pericardial effusion
TNE: serial monitoring + response to intervention — NOT a screening tool for structural CHD (Singh 2021)
Training: no national US consensus; institution-based; postfellowship advanced hemodynamics programs available
2.2 Lung POCUS

Lung Ultrasound Findings — Pathologies Identifiable Bedside
Pneumothorax: absence of lung sliding + lung point + absence of B-lines + stratosphere/barcode sign. Raimondi 2015: lung US superior to clinical evaluation and faster than CXR for pneumothorax in critically ill neonates. TTN: diffuse B-lines (comet tails). RDS: diffuse B-lines with absent A-lines; white lung pattern. MAS: heterogeneous pattern, areas of consolidation. Pleural effusion: anechoic fluid above diaphragm.
2.3 POCUS — Other Applications
Neuroimaging POCUS: identify IVH during acute decompensation; limited for subtle changes → comprehensive cranial US or MRI for accurate grading
Abdominal POCUS: free fluid (paracentesis guidance); bladder volume + urinary retention; suprapubic aspiration guidance
Vascular access POCUS: UVC/PICC position confirmation — agrees well with CXR (Zaghloul 2019); ↓ time for placement, ↓ line manipulations, ↓ radiographs (Fleming 2011; Katheria 2013)
Lumbar puncture: static (mark site) or dynamic (real-time guidance) approach — useful adjuvant tool
3. Chest Imaging
3.1 Normal Chest Radiograph
AP projection, infant supine; arms extended up/away; head neutral; chin visible top of exposure
Normal: symmetric radiolucent lungs; vessels taper to periphery; heart <60% of thoracic diameter on AP; distinct heart borders + diaphragm
Normal thymus: visible in most newborns; asymmetric, bilobar — 'wavy' lateral borders can silhouette heart (mimic cardiomegaly); asymmetric enlargement can mimic lung disease
3.2 Catheters, Tubes, Lines — Position Criteria

rows: Between medial clavicle ends and carina (mid-trachea)
RA/IVC junction (tip directed superiorly, through ductus venosus → hepatic vein → IVC)
Liver / portal vein → thrombosis, portal HTN (most common UVC complication)
Malposition → aortic branch thrombosis, ischemic injury
Near origin of innominate artery (via common carotid)

blocks.text: 3.3 Respiratory Disease: Medically Treated
Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS)
CXR: finely granular / ground-glass pattern; ↓ lung volumes; bilateral symmetric. Severe: loss of heart border + diaphragm definition; air bronchograms
Changes appear at birth or over first 6–12 hours; uncomplicated RDS resolves by day 3–4, faster with surfactant

Surfactant response: 80–90% improvement in ≥1 lung within hours. Asymmetric response → maldistribution OR insufficient surfactant. No improvement → diagnosis other than surfactant deficiency (poor prognostic sign)
Severe initial CXR predicts CPAP failure in ELBW infants (Tagliaferro 2015)
Air Leaks — PIE, Pneumomediastinum, Pneumothorax, Pneumopericardium

Air Leak Syndromes — CXR Signs ที่ต้องจำ
PIE (Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema): small rounded/linear lucencies along bronchovascular sheaths — meandering radiolucencies; diffuse or localized. Pseudocysts/pneumatoceles in interstitium. Pneumomediastinum: (1) lateral displacement mediastinal pleura; (2) continuous diaphragm sign; (3) thymic sail sign / spinnaker sail / angel wings = superior thymus elevation. Pneumothorax: (1) ↑ lucency; (2) visible visceral pleural line; (3) ↑ sharpness mediastinal border / hemidiaphragm; (4) deep costophrenic sulcus. Large PTX → contralateral mediastinal shift + ipsilateral hemidiaphragm depression/eversion. Pneumopericardium: 'halo sign' = continuous band of air conforming to cardiac contour, does NOT extend beyond great vessels — life-threatening emergency.
Other Medically Treated Respiratory Conditions
Neonatal pneumonia: diffuse granular/ground-glass (indistinguishable from RDS) OR coarse nodular/streaky; pleural effusion → suspect bacterial infection (uncommon in RDS or viral pneumonia)
TTN: streaky perihilar opacities + hyperinflation ± small pleural effusions; normal heart size; resolves 24–48 hours; often clinical diagnosis
MAS: coarse/patchy/nodular opacities + segmental hyperinflation; bilateral asymmetric; ± pleural effusion; complications: chemical pneumonitis, surfactant inactivation, PPHN, air leaks
Pulmonary hemorrhage: focal or diffuse ground-glass; may mimic pneumonia/edema; usually resolves within 24–48 hours

BPD: hyperinflated lungs; asymmetric coarse patchy opacities; bubbly cystic emphysematous changes. Bubbly/cystic changes at 28 days → best predictor of home oxygen therapy (Arai 2020; n>8000 infants); without bubbly changes: only 2% needed home O2. CT: tracheobronchomalacia in 10–48% at bronchoscopy
3.4 Respiratory Disease: Surgically Treated
Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH)

CXR: gas-filled bowel loops in thorax (initially fluid-filled → difficult); ↓ bowel in abdomen; contralateral mediastinal shift (virtually always); ipsilateral lung hypoplastic → contralateral lung also hypoplastic
Left-sided most common; partial aeration may occur with smaller hernia → difficult to distinguish from complete eventration → US confirms hemidiaphragm continuity
Prenatal: fetal MRI + US predict survival by assessing degree of pulmonary hypoplasia and associated anomalies
Congenital Pulmonary Airway Malformation (CPAM)

CPAM Classification — Stocker System
Type 0: acinar dysplasia (tracheal/bronchial origin) — incompatible with life. Type 1 (most common): single or multiple large cysts (>2 cm) of bronchial/bronchiolar origin. Type 2: small cysts (≤2 cm) of bronchiolar origin. Type 3: predominantly solid with microcysts (<0.5 cm) of bronchiolar-alveolar duct origin. Type 4: large air-filled cysts, distal acinar origin — indistinguishable from pleuropulmonary blastoma on imaging. CXR/CT: range from large air-filled cystic structures to solid consolidation. CT angiography (2D/3D): gold standard for parenchymal + vascular anatomy; essential to rule out systemic vascular supply (hybrid with sequestration). BPS: no connection to tracheobronchial tree or pulmonary arteries; systemic feeding vessel from aorta; extralobar or intralobar.

CLO (Congenital Lobar Overinflation): progressive overinflation of 1+ lobes — most common: left upper lobe > right middle > right upper. Initial CXR: opaque (retained fluid); later: hyperlucency + ↑ volume + attenuated vasculature + mediastinal shift. CT differentiates multilobar, extrinsic compression

EA/TEF: most common type: proximal EA + distal TEF (>80% of cases). CXR: blind-ending air-filled proximal esophageal pouch; air in GI tract confirms distal TEF; associated anomalies: VACTERL — all infants need further imaging for associated anomalies
4. Cardiac Imaging
CXR: rarely leads to specific diagnosis; primarily excludes pulmonary conditions as cause of respiratory distress; cardiac size >60% of thoracic diameter = cardiomegaly
Echocardiography: primary diagnostic tool for CHD; POCUS supplements (not replaces) for real-time physiologic/hemodynamic information

CTA: recommended for coronary arteries, vascular rings, pulmonary/airway anomalies, aortic arch anomalies (coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, vascular ring/sling). 320-MDCT prospective ECG-gated: low dose, free-breathing, no sedation — high diagnostic accuracy (Jadhav 2015)
MRI/MRA: gold standard for ventricular function, flow measurement, shunt quantification, valvular disease, cardiac masses, postoperative evaluation (e.g., LPA stenosis after TOF repair)
5. Abdominal Imaging
5.1 Normal AXR + Gas Pattern
Gas distribution: stomach within minutes; most small bowel by 3 hours; most large bowel by 8–9 hours of life
Normal bowel loops: polygonal shape. Central air-filled loops in absence of normal distribution → ascites. Paucity of gas → fluid-filled bowel OR obstruction
Free air detection: cross-table lateral (infant supine) or left lateral decubitus — better than supine AP for small volumes
5.2 Gastrointestinal Tract — Intestinal Obstruction

High vs Low Intestinal Obstruction — AXR Pattern
High obstruction (proximal to distal ileum): few dilated bowel loops on AXR → UGI series is the next step. Diagnoses: esophageal atresia (absent stomach bubble), duodenal atresia ('double bubble' = distended stomach + duodenal bulb), midgut malrotation (UGI → corkscrew sign). Low obstruction (distal ileum/colon): many dilated loops on AXR → contrast enema is the next step. Diagnoses: meconium ileus (microcolon + soap-bubble in RLQ), Hirschsprung disease (transition zone), ileal atresia (microcolon + reflux shows level), small left colon syndrome, anorectal malformation.
Midgut malrotation: UGI = study of choice (Taylor 2011); duodenum fails to cross midline; 'corkscrew sign'; CT also diagnostic
Duodenal atresia: 'double bubble' (distended stomach + duodenal bulb); 30% associated with trisomy 21; associated with VACTERL; water-soluble contrast confirms level

Meconium ileus: earliest clinical manifestation of cystic fibrosis; soap-bubble appearance in RLQ (gas + thick meconium); contrast enema → microcolon; enema can be therapeutic (contrast reflux into ileum). Calcified meconium in abdomen/scrotum → meconium peritonitis

Hirschsprung disease: low obstruction; contrast enema shows transition zone (narrow aganglionic segment → dilated normal segment); rectosigmoid ratio <1 = normal; reverse ratio → Hirschsprung; barium retention at 24h
5.3 Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC)

NEC Imaging — Progression Stages
Early: nonspecific — bowel dilation + rounded/elongated loops; focal/asymmetric fixed loops. Pneumatosis intestinalis: 'bubbly' pattern or curvilinear lucencies in bowel wall — pathognomonic; RLQ most common. Portal venous gas: branching linear lucencies overlying liver — later finding; may be transient; does NOT always correlate with disease severity. Pneumoperitoneum: 'football sign' (large supine pneumoperitoneum — air outlines falciform ligament). Left lateral decubitus/cross-table lateral: free air = bowel perforation → urgent surgical consultation. AUS: bowel wall thickening + echogenic free fluid → ↑ surgical NEC risk independently (Lazow 2021); portal venous gas as intraluminal echogenic foci within liver.
NEC strictures: delayed complication; 20–30% of NEC survivors; single or multiple; usually colon at splenic flexure; water-soluble contrast enema for diagnosis
5.4 Hepatobiliary Tract
Biliary Atresia

US: primary exam for neonatal jaundice; gallbladder <15–19 mm (fasted ≥3 h) → suggestive; absent/abnormal GB in ~80%; triangular cord sign (echogenic fibrous remnant >3–4 mm along right portal vein): 84% sensitive, 100% specific with GB abnormality (Napolitano 2021)
Hepatobiliary scintigraphy: highly sensitive; phenobarbital pretreatment ↑ specificity; GI excretion excludes biliary atresia; lack of excretion by 24h → highly suggestive of atresia
Cholangiogram: gold standard — absent gallbladder lumen, atretric GB, or absent contrast in biliary system + small bowel; diagnosis and pre-Kasai planning
Choledochal Cyst
Congenital cystic dilation of extrahepatic or intrahepatic biliary tree; Todani classification (I–V) by location/morphology
US: initial imaging; MR cholangiography: gold standard — confirms biliary origin + preoperative planning
Always rule out biliary atresia given clinical overlap and association
Cholelithiasis & Hepatic Calcifications

Neonatal cholelithiasis: 10% of pediatric gallstones in first 6 months; TPN → gallstones in up to 40%; also furosemide, dehydration, hemolytic anemia, short-gut; sonographic: rounded mobile echogenic focus in GB

Hepatic calcifications: often idiopathic; intrauterine infections (CMV, toxoplasmosis, rubella, HSV, syphilis, varicella) → hepatic calcifications + ascites; subcapsular → UVC complications (emboli); perihepatic → meconium peritonitis
Hepatoblastoma: presents as right hepatic lobe mass; heterogeneous enhancement; MRI best characterization; may present in neonatal period
6. Urinary Tract Imaging
6.1 Normal Neonatal Kidney on US
↑ parenchymal echogenicity (neonates) due to high glomerular density + cellularity — isoechoic or hyperechoic vs liver/spleen; normalizes at 3–4 months
Conspicuously hypoechoic pyramids: larger medullary volume; persists up to 1 year — do NOT confuse with dilated collecting system
Transient ↑ medullary echogenicity at birth: diffuse, bilateral; resolves within 10 days — normal finding
6.2 Urinary Tract Dilatation (UTD Classification)

UTD Classification System — Postnatal Management
UTD Classification: multidisciplinary consensus (urology/nephrology/radiology). APRPD <10 mm + no calyceal or ureteral dilatation → NOT at increased risk; no follow-up needed (Nelson 2019). Ureteral diameter >4 mm = abnormal. UTD P1 (low risk): central calyceal dilation — similar outcomes to normal; no ↑ significant uropathy. UTD P2/P3: evaluate with VCUG. UTD grading predicts clinical outcomes with similar/better reliability than other systems (Nguyen 2022). 70–80% of prenatal UTD is transient/physiologic.
Causes of significant UTD: UPJ obstruction (most common), vesicoureteral reflux, PUV/bladder outlet obstruction, ureterocele, UVJ obstruction/nonrefluxing megaureter
6.3 Obstructive Uropathy

UPJ obstruction: most common urinary tract obstruction + flank mass. US: dilated intrarenal collecting system WITHOUT ureteral dilation; bilateral in 25–33%; associated with contralateral MCDK (→ prompt intervention to prevent renal failure). MAG3 renal scan: quantify degree of obstruction + differential renal function

Posterior Urethral Valves (PUV): most common cause of lower urinary tract obstruction; leading cause of end-stage renal disease in boys. VCUG: dilated posterior urethra + diminished caliber distal to valves; bilateral hydroureteronephrosis + thick trabeculated bladder. Dysplastic renal changes: ↑ echogenicity + loss of corticomedullary differentiation + cortical cysts

MCDK: pelvic infundibular atresia; multiple noncommunicating cysts of variable size + absent renal parenchyma/collecting system; natural history: gradual involution over months–years; up to 50% have contralateral renal abnormality (UPJ obstruction, VUR)
ARPKD: symmetric markedly enlarged hyperechoic kidneys (↑ echogenicity from cystic dilation of collecting ducts); hypoechoic rim = compressed cortex; associated periportal hepatic fibrosis
6.4 Renal Vein Thrombosis, Nephrocalcinosis

RVT: acute: ↑ kidney size + ↑ parenchymal echogenicity with echogenic striations + loss of CMD; Doppler: ↓ perfusion, high-resistance arterial flow, reversal of diastolic flow; risk factors: hemoconcentration, sepsis, maternal DM, indwelling catheter

Nephrocalcinosis: medullary pyramids most commonly affected; furosemide therapy = most common cause in NICU; also hypervitaminosis D, distal RTA. US: ↑ echogenicity in normally hypoechoic pyramids (initially without shadowing); resolves after stopping furosemide
6.5 Adrenal Glands

Adrenal hemorrhage: most common neonatal adrenal mass; right > left (shorter right adrenal vein); ↑ risk: perinatal asphyxia, sepsis, coagulopathy, hypotension, surgery. US: oval/triangular solid mass → calcifications weeks–months later; ↓ size over 1–2 weeks on follow-up (DDx from neuroblastoma)

Neuroblastoma: most common neonatal neoplasm; most from adrenal gland; 123I-MIBG: first-line functional imaging; uptake in 90% of neuroblastomas; CT/MRI for staging + intraspinal extension. 18F-FDG PET CT for MIBG-weak/negative tumors
7. Brain Imaging
7.1 Cranial Ultrasound — Technique
Through anterior fontanelle: coronal + sagittal views; assess gray-white matter, extra-axial space, meninges, dural sinuses, ventricles, gyral morphology
Mastoid fontanelle views: best for tentorium, brainstem, occipital horns, posterior fossa — improves cerebellar hemorrhage detection (Ibrahim 2018)
Color/power/spectral Doppler: large intracranial arteries + veins; sinovenous thrombosis, vascular malformations; microvessel imaging (novel — functional insights)
MRI: more sensitive than cranial US for cerebellar hemorrhage, PVL, congenital anomalies, HIE change, infections, neoplasms
Routine MRI screening at term-equivalent in preterm: questioned — findings do not correlate with long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes (Ho 2015)
7.2 Germinal Matrix Hemorrhage — IVH (GMH-IVH)

GMH-IVH Classification — Papile Grading System
Highest risk: GA <32 wk or BW <1500 g; most hemorrhages by first week of life. Origin: caudothalamic groove (fragile thin-walled vessels with little connective tissue). Grade 1: subependymal hemorrhage only (echogenic focus inferolateral to frontal horn). Grade 2: SEH + IVH occupying 10–50% of ventricular area (nondilated ventricle). Grade 3: SEH + IVH occupying ≥50% + ventriculomegaly. Grade 4: periventricular hemorrhagic infarction (echogenic periventricular abnormalities = venous infarct from compression of periventricular veins). Ventriculomegaly sequela: trigones + occipital horns dilate first. Fronto-temporal horn ratio: useful 2D quantitative biomarker for serial monitoring (Obeid 2021).
7.3 Periventricular Leukomalacia (PVL)
Infarction of deep white matter adjacent to trigones + frontal horns in preterm infants; may coexist with IVH
Early US: usually normal → earliest sign: ↑ periventricular echogenicity. Progression → parenchymal cystic areas (communicate with ventricle → ex vacuo dilatation)
MRI: most sensitive — T2 hyperintensity peritrigonal; thinned/atrophic corpus callosum; irregular lateral ventricular contour; asymmetric ventricular enlargement
7.4 Hypoxic Ischemic Encephalopathy (HIE) — Term
Cranial US: normal OR diffuse cerebral edema (slit-like ventricles)

MRI with DWI: gold standard for early diagnosis. Cytotoxic edema = high signal restricted diffusion on DWI + low ADC; appears before T1/T2 changes in acute phase. Diffusion restriction on DWI predicts poor outcome
7.5 Lenticulostriate Vasculopathy (LSV)
Echogenic vessels in basal ganglia/thalamus; incidence 15% in <32 wk (Sisman 2018)

Isolated LSV: normal neurodevelopment, no further imaging needed; associated with TORCH infections, chromosomal abnormalities, fetal alcohol syndrome, bacterial meningitis, inborn errors of metabolism, HIE
8. Spine and Skeletal Imaging
8.1 Occult Spinal Dysraphism
Indication: cutaneous stigmata (skin dimples, deviated gluteal folds) — 50–80% of occult spinal dysraphism has cutaneous lesions

Spinal US <6 months: first-line; evaluate terminal conus position, cord/nerve root motion, filum terminale, dorsal dermal sinus tract. Normal conus: superior L1 to top of L3; conus at L3 or below → MRI to confirm tethering mass/cyst
MRI: first-line >6 months (ossification of posterior elements limits US window after 6 months)
8.2 Developmental Dysplasia of Hip (DDH)
Risk factors: breech in 3rd trimester, family history, clubfoot, metatarsus adductus
US: exam of choice <6 months; best after 4 weeks (hip maturation ↑ specificity); dynamic assessment — acetabular morphology + femoral head coverage + instability under adduction stress
After 4–5 months: femoral epiphysis ossifies → obscures US landmarks → radiography becomes more reliable. AP pelvis radiograph at >6 months
8.3 Skeletal Survey and Intrauterine Infections

Intrauterine infection: congenital rubella + CMV: metaphyseal lesions with vertical striations ('celery stalk'); congenital syphilis: periostitis + radiolucent transverse metaphyseal bands ± bone destruction/fragmentation
Skeletal survey (for dysplasia): lateral skull + spine; AP upper + lower extremities, pelvis, chest, abdomen
9. NICU Practical — Bedside Actions
9.1 Reading Every NICU CXR — Systematic Approach
Step 1 — Lines/tubes first: ETT (medial clavicles to carina), enteric tube (beyond GEJ), UVC (RA/IVC junction), UAC (T6–10 or L3–5), PICC (SVC or 9th–12th ribs).
Step 2 — Lung fields: symmetry; granularity; air bronchograms; lucencies (PIE/pneumothorax).
Step 3 — Mediastinum: heart size (<60% thoracic); thymus (recognize normal asymmetry); trachea midline.
Step 4 — Pleura: pleural line visible? costophrenic angle sharp? diaphragm contour? 'halo' around heart?
Step 5 — Abdomen (on chest film): bowel gas pattern; hepatic lucencies (portal venous gas?).
9.2 Head US Timing in Preterm Infants
Standard protocol: first US at 7–10 days of life (captures most GMH-IVH); repeat at 4 weeks + at term-equivalent age; additional scans if clinical deterioration or IVH found
When to act: Grade 3–4 IVH → ↑ risk post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus; monitor head circumference + ventricular size serially; neurosurgery consult for progressive ventriculomegaly
Mastoid views: request when cerebellar hemorrhage suspected (extreme prematurity, birth trauma, coagulopathy) — anterior fontanelle misses posterior fossa
9.3 NEC Imaging Protocol
Serial AXR every 6h in NEC Bell stage II–III: track progression: pneumatosis → portal venous gas → pneumoperitoneum = surgical indication
Pneumatosis alert: may be transient and resolve even as disease progresses — do not reassure based on clearing pneumatosis alone; AUS + clinical picture
Football sign: air outlines entire peritoneal cavity on AP supine + falciform ligament = large pneumoperitoneum → surgery without delay
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Read Lines Before Lungs on Every CXR
ETT in right mainstem bronchus = most common malposition → right lung hyperinflation + left atelectasis. UVC in liver (portal branch) → portal vein thrombosis + portal hypertension: risk is asymmetric lung expansion of 'good' lung, not just the liver complication. UAC malposition → thrombosis of aortic branches (renal/mesenteric/celiac) → ischemic injury. สรุป: ไม่อ่าน lines ก่อน → อาจ treat lung disease ที่จริงเป็นแค่ malposition.

PEARL 2 — Thymic Sail ≠ Pneumomediastinum ไม่เสมอ; Halo = Emergency เสมอ
Thymic sail sign (spinnaker sail / angel wings) = pneumomediastinum — thymus displaced superiorly. แต่: thymus เองก็สามารถ asymmetric และ sail-shaped ได้ในทารกปกติ → ตรวจ continuous diaphragm sign + mediastinal pleura displacement. ส่วน halo sign = continuous band of air conforming to cardiac contour = pneumopericardium — cardiac tamponade ถ้า hemodynamically compromised → pericardiocentesis ทันที. ทั้งสองต้องจำสัญลักษณ์ให้แม่น.

PEARL 3 — GMH-IVH Grade 4 ≠ Always Grade 4: Periventricular Venous Infarct
Grade 4 ใน Papile system ≠ 'extension of IVH into parenchyma' — จริงๆ คือ periventricular hemorrhagic infarction จาก compression ของ periventricular veins โดย subependymal hemorrhage ที่ขยายตัว. Etiology ต่างกัน → prognosis: unilateral infarct = better prognosis กว่า bilateral. ไม่ใช่ choroid plexus hemorrhage หรือ isolated venous infarct ที่ไม่มี GMH (ซึ่งมี IVH ได้ด้วย). เวลา counsel ครอบครัว: ต้องรู้ว่า laterality + grade + presence of hydrocephalus เป็นอย่างไร.

PEARL 4 — Gonadal Shielding ไม่ใช้แล้วตั้งแต่ 2019: รู้ให้ถูกต้อง
AAPM, ACR, RSNA, Image Gently ทั้งหมดแนะนำ discontinuation of routine gonadal shielding ตั้งแต่ 2019 เพราะ: (1) shields obscure anatomy → second exposure; (2) triggered auto-exposure → ↑ dose; (3) gonads less radiosensitive than previously thought; (4) modern equipment dose ต่ำมาก. ถ้าพ่อแม่ถาม: อธิบายตามสคริปต์ AAPM FAQs — 'we have better machines; some exams cannot be done with shields; if you insist and it's possible, we will try'. อย่าใส่ shield โดยอัตโนมัติเพราะ 'เคยทำ'.

PEARL 5 — Biliary Atresia Workup: Triangular Cord Sign + Fasted GB + Scintigraphy
Workup sequence ใน conjugated hyperbilirubinemia: (1) US fasted ≥3h — GB <15–19 mm + triangular cord sign >3–4 mm = 84% sensitive, 100% specific สำหรับ biliary atresia (vs INH) (Napolitano 2021); (2) hepatobiliary scintigraphy หลัง phenobarbital pretreatment — GI excretion rules out BA; no excretion at 24h = highly suggestive; (3) cholangiogram = gold standard ก่อน Kasai. ถ้าทำ workup ช้า → Kasai หลัง 8 wk → native liver transplant-free survival ↓ อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ.
11. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Ebenebe CU et al J Perinatol 2019;39:115: digital radiographic effective dose in VLBW infants: 0.012–0.016 mSv per radiograph; total NICU stay exposure < annual natural background
Cameron JR Environ Health Perspect 1991;91:45: BERT relative risk descriptor for radiation communication; newborn CXR ≈ 1 day background radiation
AAPM Policy PP 32-A (2019): discontinuation of routine gonadal/fetal shielding in diagnostic imaging — no benefit, tangible risks
Singh Y et al Crit Care 2020;24:65: ESPNIC international evidence-based guidelines for POCUS in critically ill neonates and children — standardizes indications
Raimondi F et al J Pediatr 2015;175:74: lung US superior to clinical evaluation + faster than CXR for pneumothorax diagnosis in critically ill neonates
Fleming SE & Kim JH J Perinatol 2011;31:344: POCUS-guided umbilical catheter insertion: ↓ time, manipulations, radiographs vs conventional method
Katheria AC et al J Perinatol 2013;33:791 (RCT): US-guided PICC vs standard CXR in neonates: improved success, fewer manipulations
Thiim KR et al J Perinatol 2022: in-NICU MRI system: diagnostic yield similar to conventional 3T MRI; avoids transport risks for critically ill neonates
Jadhav SP et al AJR 2015;204:W184: 320-MDCT prospective ECG-gated CTA in neonates: low radiation, free-breathing, high diagnostic accuracy for cardiac anatomy
Tagliaferro T et al J Perinatol 2015;35:99: severe RDS on initial CXR predicts CPAP failure in ELBW infants — imaging useful for ventilatory planning
Arai H et al Acta Paediatr 2020;109:711: bubbly/cystic changes at 28 days of life in extreme preterm: strongest predictor of home oxygen therapy (>GA, BW, chorioamnionitis)
Lazow SP et al Am J Surg 2021;222:1034: AUS bowel wall thickening + echogenic free fluid independently predict surgical NEC; AUS adds diagnostic utility beyond radiography alone
Napolitano M et al Pediatr Radiol 2021;51:314: triangular cord sign + GB abnormality on US: 84% sensitivity, 100% specificity for biliary atresia; US algorithm for neonatal jaundice
Nguyen HT et al Pediatr Radiol 2022;52:740: 2021 UTD classification update: P1 (central calyceal dilation) has similar outcomes to normal — no ↑ uropathy risk; P2/P3 need VCUG

Sisman J et al J Perinatol 2018;38:1370: LSV in preterm <32 wk: 15% incidence; isolated LSV = normal neurodevelopment; new classification system; associated conditions include TORCH, bacterial meningitis
Ibrahim J et al Pediatr Res 2018;84:799: clinical review + algorithm for cranial US vs MRI in <32 wk preterm: US for routine surveillance; MRI for PVL assessment + complex pathology
Obeid R et al Pediatr Res 2021;89:1715: fronto-temporal horn ratio: reliable 2D quantitative biomarker for ventriculomegaly in preterm; standardizes serial ventricular measurements
Stocker JT Fetal Pediatr Pathol 2009;28:155: CPAM classification types 0–4; type 4 indistinguishable from pleuropulmonary blastoma — surveillance required
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: UAC 'high position' vs 'low position' คืออะไร และ malposition มีผลอะไร?

ตอบ: High: T6–T10 (above visceral arteries — celiac, SMA, renal). Low: L3–L5 (below visceral arteries). Tip directed superiorly in aorta. Malposition → aortic branch thrombosis → ischemia ของ bowel, kidney, spinal cord. UAC path: umbilical artery → internal iliac → common iliac → aorta (tip superiorly). ต้องแยกจาก UVC ซึ่งเดินทางขึ้น via portal vein + ductus venosus.
Q: PIE บน CXR มีลักษณะอย่างไร และต่างจาก pneumothorax ยังไง?

ตอบ: PIE: small rounded or linear lucencies along bronchovascular sheaths — meandering radiolucencies; diffuse or localized; ไม่มี pleural line. เกิดจาก alveolar rupture → interstitial air. Pneumothorax: visible visceral pleural line; ↑ overall lucency; deep costophrenic sulcus; mediastinal shift ถ้า tension. PIE อาจ dissect → pneumomediastinum หรือ pneumothorax. Pseudocysts/pneumatoceles = larger focal interstitial air collections.
Q: NEC: pneumatosis intestinalis vs portal venous gas — ความหมายทางคลินิกต่างกันอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Pneumatosis intestinalis: intramural gas in bowel wall — pathognomonic ของ NEC; 'bubbly' หรือ curvilinear lucencies; มักเริ่มที่ RLQ. Portal venous gas: branching linear lucencies overlying liver — later finding, more severe NEC; may be TRANSIENT — อย่าสบายใจเมื่อหายไป (disease อาจยังดำเนินอยู่). ทั้งคู่ไม่ได้ correlate perfectly กับ clinical severity — ต้องดู clinical + AUS + ประเมิน ruptured bowel (→ pneumoperitoneum).
Q: GMH-IVH: ทำไม Grade 4 ถึง worse prognosis กว่า Grade 3?

ตอบ: Grade 4 = periventricular hemorrhagic infarction (ไม่ใช่แค่ ventricle เต็มไปด้วยเลือด). Mechanism: subependymal hemorrhage ขนาดใหญ่ compress periventricular veins → venous infarction → white matter destruction. Consequence: unilateral periventricular infarct → contralateral hemiplegia + developmental delay. Bilateral → more severe outcome. Grade 3: ventriculomegaly เกิดจาก clot/debris obstruct → post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus risk → neurosurgery (reservoir/shunt); parenchyma intact ถ้าไม่มี infarct.
Q: Biliary atresia vs neonatal hepatitis: workup ที่ทำ ต้องการข้อมูลอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: US fasted ≥3h: GB size (normal 15–30 mm), shape, wall; triangular cord sign (>3–4 mm echogenic cord at porta hepatis) — 84% sensitive, 100% specific with GB abnormality. Hepatobiliary scintigraphy (หลัง phenobarbital 5 mg/kg/day x 5 วัน): GI excretion within 24h = BA excluded; no excretion = highly suggestive BA. MRCP: noninvasive biliary duct mapping. Cholangiogram = gold standard. สำคัญ: Kasai before 8 wk of age → native liver transplant-free survival เพิ่มขึ้นมาก — diagnosis และ referral ต้องรวดเร็ว.
Q: VCUG สั่งเมื่อใดใน postnatal urinary tract dilatation workup?

ตอบ: UTD P2 หรือ P3 (ตาม UTD classification 2021 update) → VCUG. UTD P1 (central calyceal dilation เท่านั้น) หรือ normal (APRPD <10 mm, no calyceal/ureteral dilation) → ไม่จำเป็นต้องทำ VCUG ทันที; outcomes similar (Nelson 2019). Ureteral diameter >4 mm = abnormal. VCUG indications others: UPJ obstruction (associated VUR 30%); PUV confirmation (dilated posterior urethra = diagnostic); recurrent UTI. Timing: สำหรับ low-grade UTD ที่ไม่มี febrile UTI — US ซ้ำที่ 4–6 wk อาจเพียงพอก่อน VCUG.
Q: Head US ใน HIE term infant: US ปกติหมายความว่า brain ปกติหรือไม่?

ตอบ: ไม่ใช่ — cranial US อาจ normal หรือ show only nonspecific diffuse edema (slit-like ventricles) ใน acute HIE. MRI with DWI เป็น gold standard สำหรับ early HIE detection: cytotoxic edema = ↑ signal DWI + ↓ ADC — appears BEFORE T1/T2 changes (ช่วง first 24–96h). Diffusion restriction on DWI predicts poor outcome. ดังนั้น: US ปกติใน HIE term infant → ไม่ rule out significant brain injury → MRI with DWI ที่ 24–96h of life.
Q: Spinal US สำหรับ occult spinal dysraphism: ทำในเด็กอายุเท่าไหร่และ normal conus position อยู่ที่ไหน?

ตอบ: US: first-line ใน <6 months (ossification ของ posterior elements ยัง minimal → sonic window ดี). Normal conus: superior border L1 ถึง top of L3. Conus at L3 หรือต่ำกว่า → MRI เพื่อยืนยัน tethering + characterize mass/cyst. >6 months: MRI is first-line (posterior elements ossify → US unreliable). Cutaneous stigmata ที่ high risk: skin dimple ≥2.5 cm above anal margin, deviated gluteal fold, subcutaneous mass/lipoma, hemangioma overlying spine, tethered tuft of hair.

Day 136 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.39
บทต่อไป: Chapter 39 — Anesthesia in the Neonate. เตรียมตัว: pharmacology ของ anesthetic agents ใน neonate; neurotoxicity controversy ของ general anesthesia ใน early life (FDA 2016 drug safety communication); regional anesthesia techniques (caudal, spinal, epidural) ใน neonatal surgery; pain assessment tools (CRIES, NIPS, NFCS); sedation for procedures: morphine, fentanyl, midazolam, dexmedetomidine, ketamine — dosing + pharmacokinetics in neonates; neuromuscular blockade agents; anesthesia for ex utero intrapartum treatment (EXIT); temperature management during anesthesia.
